items.html:
Knowledge is only half the training

This course teaches the knowledge element of moving and handling — the law, the principles and the reasons behind safe practice. It cannot make you competent on its own. Safe moving and handling needs practical, hands-on training. Before you move any person or any load as part of your work, you must also complete practical training and be assessed as competent by a qualified trainer or assessor. Only ever use equipment you have been trained to use; if you have not been shown how to use a piece of equipment, do not use it.

With that in mind, this page explains why technique matters so much. The part of you most at risk is your back.

Your spine and how it gets hurt

Your spine is a column of small bones called vertebrae, cushioned by soft discs and held together by muscles and ligaments. It is strong when you keep its natural curves and work close to your body, but it is easily overloaded. Back injuries in care rarely come from one dramatic lift; more often they build up over months of small, awkward strains.

  • Bending and twisting: loading the spine while it is curved or turned puts great strain on the discs.
  • Reaching and stooping: holding weight away from your body multiplies the force through your back.
  • Repetition and fatigue: the same task done many times, or when you are tired, wears the back down.

Damage such as a slipped disc or long-term back pain can end a career, which is exactly why the Manual Handling Operations Regulations 1992 exist.

items.question: Most back injuries in care work are caused by a single heavy lift going wrong.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Most back damage builds up gradually from repeated bending, twisting, stooping and reaching, often while tired, rather than from one dramatic lift.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. This is false: back injuries usually build up over time from repeated awkward movements, not from a single heavy lift.

items.html:
The main types of equipment

Using the right equipment is how you avoid hazardous manual handling. Each piece has a purpose, and you should only use one you have been trained on.

  • Mobile and overhead hoists: take the full weight of a person who cannot bear their own weight, lifting them with a sling.
  • Slings: the fabric part that holds the person; they come in different types and sizes and must match the hoist and the person.
  • Slide sheets: low-friction sheets that let you reposition someone in bed without dragging them.
  • Stand aids and standing hoists: support a person who can take some weight to rise and transfer.
  • Transfer boards: bridge a small gap, for example between a wheelchair and a bed, for someone who can shuffle across.

Checks and LOLER

Equipment that lifts people is covered by the Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations 1998 (LOLER). A hoist and its slings must be thoroughly examined by a competent person — usually every six months for equipment that lifts a person. This is on top of routine servicing.

Before every use, you carry out your own visual check:

  • Sling: look for fraying, tears, worn stitching and that the label with its size and safe working load is readable.
  • Hoist: check the battery, brakes, spreader bar and that nothing is damaged or overdue for examination.
  • Suitability: confirm the sling type and size are the ones named in the person’s handling plan.

If anything is worn, damaged or out of test date, take it out of use, label it and report the fault. Never make do with a sling that is the wrong size or looks unsafe.

items.question: You are about to hoist someone and notice the sling’s stitching is frayed near one of the loops. What should you do?

answers.text: Take the sling out of use, report the fault and use a sound sling of the correct type and size.
answers.feedback: Correct. A damaged sling could fail and drop the person; it must be removed from use and reported, and only a sound, suitable sling used.

answers.text: Use it carefully this once, as the person is waiting and it is only slightly frayed.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Frayed stitching can give way under load; a sling in that state must never be used, however slight the damage looks.

answers.text: Turn the sling around so the frayed part is at the back.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Repositioning the damage does not make the sling safe; it still bears the person’s weight and could fail.

answers.text: Reinforce it with tape and carry on.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Improvised repairs are never acceptable on lifting equipment; the sling must be withdrawn and reported.

items.html:
Principles for common tasks

The moves you do most often are the ones most likely to catch you out. The principles below apply to all of them — but reading about a task is never a substitute for being shown it and practising under a trainer before you do it for real.

  • Plan first: read the person’s handling plan, clear the space, and check any equipment.
  • Encourage independence: let the person do what they safely can; you assist, you do not take over.
  • Keep close and stable: work near your body, keep a stable base and move your feet instead of twisting.
  • Communicate: explain what will happen and agree a clear signal so you move together.

Three tasks you will meet often

These are the everyday moves where technique and equipment matter most.

  • Sit-to-stand: the person shuffles to the edge of the seat, leans forward ‘nose over toes’ and rises using the chair arms; you guide and steady rather than pull them up.
  • Repositioning in bed: use slide sheets and, where needed, a profiling bed to move someone up or turn them, so you never drag them by hand.
  • Bed to chair: for someone who can weight-bear, a stand aid or a well-planned assisted transfer; for someone who cannot, a hoist.

If the plan calls for a hoist, use a hoist. Choosing a quicker manual method to save time is exactly how both you and the person get hurt.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on everyday moving tasks.
items.textField: To help someone stand, ask them to shuffle forward and lean ‘nose over *toes*’ before rising. To move a person up or turn them in bed, use *slide* sheets so you never drag them. Where the handling plan calls for a *hoist*, you must use one rather than a quicker manual method. Throughout any task you should encourage the person’s *independence* and let them do what they safely can. Always move your *feet* rather than twisting your spine.

items.html:
If a person slips or falls during a move

Even with good planning, a person may slip, lose their balance or begin to fall while you are assisting them. You cannot safely catch a falling adult — trying to do so is one of the commonest ways carers injure their backs, and it can hurt the person too.

If someone starts to fall, do not fight it. Protect their head as best you can, guide them gently towards the floor or back into a chair, and lower yourself with them, keeping your own posture as safe as you can. A controlled slide to the floor is far safer than a sudden grab or a struggle to hold them upright.

After a fall: never lift from the floor by hand

Once a person is on the floor, do not lift them manually and do not let them be dragged or hauled up by their arms.

  • Check them first: stay calm, keep them still and warm, and look for injury, pain or a change in how they seem.
  • Get help: if they are hurt, unwell or you are unsure, call for medical help or an ambulance and follow your falls procedure.
  • Use the right equipment: if it is safe to lift them, use a suitable hoist — often a specialist raising or floor hoist — not your own strength.
  • Record and report: write an accurate account and report the fall so the cause can be reviewed.

A person who has fallen may have a fracture or a head injury that is not obvious, so a manual lift risks serious harm.

items.question: During an assisted walk, Mr Price’s knees give way and he sinks to the floor. He is conscious but complains his hip hurts. What is the safest response?

answers.text: Keep him still and reassured, check for injury, call for medical help, and use a hoist rather than lifting him by hand.
answers.feedback: Correct. A hip that hurts after a fall may be fractured; he should not be moved by hand, and any lift should use suitable equipment after he has been checked.

answers.text: Lift him under the arms back onto his feet before he gets cold.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Hauling him up by the arms could worsen a possible fracture and injure your own back; he must not be lifted manually.

answers.text: Ask him to push himself up quickly while you pull on his hands.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Pulling him up risks his injury and yours; he needs checking and, if lifted, a hoist rather than manual effort.

answers.text: Leave him where he is and carry on with your other tasks until help is free.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. He must not be left unattended; stay with him, keep him warm and safe, and summon help straight away.

items.html:
When needs are more complex

Some people need extra thought before you move them, and their handling plan should set out exactly how.

  • Bariatric needs: a person of larger body size may need specialist bariatric equipment rated for a higher safe working load, more space and often more staff. Standard equipment used beyond its safe working load can fail.
  • Unpredictable movement: someone who is distressed, confused, has spasms or reacts to pain may move suddenly. Plan for it, keep clear of harm, and never rush.
  • Pain and fragile skin: conditions such as arthritis, recent surgery, contractures or fragile skin change how a person can be handled and how much they can help.

Risk assessments change

A handling plan is based on a TILE(O) assessment — the Task, the Individual, the Load or person, the Environment, and Other factors such as equipment. None of these stays fixed. A person’s health, weight, pain, mood and skill can change day to day, and so can the space and equipment around them.

This is why you also make a dynamic risk assessment in the moment: before every move, check that the plan still fits what you see today. If the person is more unwell, more agitated or in more pain than usual, or if the equipment or environment is not right, stop. Do not force a move that no longer feels safe. Report the change so the written assessment can be reviewed and, in line with Care Certificate Standard 13.3, agree a safe way forward before continuing.

items.question: Mrs Devi normally does a stand-aid transfer, but today she is drowsy, in pain and cannot grip the handles. Her plan still says stand aid. What should you do?

answers.text: Stop, do not force the stand-aid transfer, keep her comfortable and report the change so her assessment can be reviewed.
answers.feedback: Correct. Your dynamic risk assessment shows the plan no longer fits today; the safe course is to pause, keep her safe and get the plan reviewed.

answers.text: Carry on with the stand aid because that is what the written plan says.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A plan is a starting point, not a rule to follow blindly; if the person cannot safely use the stand aid today, using it could injure you both.

answers.text: Lift her across yourself just this once as she seems too weak for the stand aid.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A manual lift is hazardous and controversial practice; the answer to an unsafe stand aid is never to lift by hand.

answers.text: Give her some time and then insist she grips the handles and stands.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Pressing on with a move she cannot safely manage risks a fall and injury; the situation needs stopping and reviewing.
